title: Bible John: Glasgow's Dance Hall Killer 🔍

segments.voice: Between February 1968 and October 1969, three young women were murdered after dancing at the Barrowland Ballroom in Glasgow, Scotland. The killer was tall, well-dressed, polite, and reportedly quoted scripture during conversations. Witnesses called him Bible John. He was never identified.

The Barrowland Ballroom was a popular Glasgow dance hall in the 1960s. Friday and Saturday nights drew hundreds of young Scots looking to dance. The hall was known for an atmosphere of relative respectability — many married women attended without husbands.

On February 23, 1968, Patricia Docker, 25, was found strangled in a lane near her home in the Battlefield area of Glasgow. She had been at the Barrowland the previous night. Her body had been left in a position that suggested deliberate placement.

Glasgow's CID — Criminal Investigation Department — investigated. They identified some witnesses who had seen Docker dancing with a tall man earlier in the evening. Descriptions were limited. The CID had no significant leads.

On August 16, 1969, 18 months after Docker's death, Jemima McDonald, 32, was found murdered in a derelict tenement on Mackeith Street, near her Bridgeton home. She had been at the Barrowland earlier that evening. Her body had been arranged similarly to Docker's.

Glasgow CID Detective Inspector Joe Beattie led the investigation. He connected the two killings based on the dance-hall pattern, the strangulation method, and the body positioning. He began appealing publicly for witnesses from the Barrowland.

On October 30, 1969, Helen Puttock, 29, was murdered after a night at the Barrowland. Her body was found in a back court behind her home in Scotstoun. She had been strangled. Her body had been left exposed but in a position consistent with the previous victims.

Here's where the case had its decisive break — and where it failed. Helen Puttock had been with her sister Jeannie Williams that night. Jeannie had ridden in a taxi with Helen and a man Helen had been dancing with. The man had introduced himself only as 'John.'

Jeannie had heard the man speak at length during the taxi ride. He had quoted from the Bible — references to Moses, to the prophets, to the wages of sin. He had spoken with what Jeannie described as 'a posh accent' for working-class Glasgow.

Jeannie gave investigators an unusually detailed description. The man was tall — about 6 foot 1 — with light auburn hair, blue or grey eyes, and a slight overbite. He had distinctive teeth — one tooth slightly out of line. He was well-dressed in a stylish suit.

Glasgow CID developed a composite sketch from Jeannie's description. The sketch was distributed widely — in Scottish newspapers, on television, and in posters throughout Glasgow. It became one of the most widely-circulated police images in British history.

Despite the strong description and the wide circulation, no arrest came. Detective Inspector Beattie spent the next decade pursuing the case. He interviewed thousands of men. He kept a personal file. He maintained contact with Jeannie Williams.
The killings stopped after Puttock's murder. There were no more confirmed Bible John killings. The pattern simply ceased.

In the 1990s, after Beattie's death, Strathclyde Police reviewed the case. They identified a person of interest: John Irvine McInnes, a Scotsman who had killed himself in 1980. McInnes had matched the general physical description and had been at the Barrowland during the relevant period.

In 1996, McInnes's body was exhumed and DNA was extracted. The DNA was compared to a stain found on the clothing of one Bible John victim. The result was inconclusive — not a clear match, not a clear exclusion.

Public reaction to the McInnes exhumation was mixed. Some saw it as appropriate investigative work. Others viewed it as a violation of a man whose involvement could not be conclusively established. The case did not formally close.

Some researchers have argued that Bible John was actually Peter Tobin, a Scottish serial killer convicted of three murders in the late 2000s. Tobin had been the right age, had worked in Glasgow during the relevant period, and had murdered women across multiple British locations.
Tobin denied being Bible John before his death in 2022. DNA from Bible John victims was compared to Tobin's profile. The results have not been publicly stated to be a definitive match.
Some retired investigators continue to support the Tobin theory. Others argue that the geographic pattern and physical description point to a different offender entirely.
The Barrowland Ballroom still stands and operates as a music venue. Its connection to the Bible John case is well-known in Glasgow history. Local guides occasionally include it in walking tours.

Police Scotland — successor to Strathclyde Police — maintains the original Bible John case file. The file includes thousands of statements, the Jeannie Williams account, the original composite sketches, and the McInnes exhumation results.
If new DNA technology becomes available that can produce a clearer profile from the original evidence, Police Scotland has stated they will pursue any new lead. The case is considered open.

Helen Puttock's sister Jeannie Williams gave additional interviews about the case in subsequent decades. Her detailed memory of the taxi conversation has remained one of the strongest pieces of evidence in the file.

The Bible John case is studied in British criminology as a major example of how a confident witness description can fail to produce an arrest in the absence of supporting forensic evidence. It is also studied as a foundational case in Scottish cold-case methodology.

If you remember anything about Glasgow's Barrowland Ballroom in 1968 or 1969, particularly any encounter with a tall, well-spoken man who quoted scripture, Police Scotland continues to accept information through its public tip line.
